input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **system_under_study** in the provided documents primarily focuses on **cyber-biophysical systems** (Document [7]) and **automotive systems** (Documents [8], [9], [12], [14], [17]), with additional references to **manufacturing systems** (Document [10]), **aerospace applications** (Document [16]), and **battery systems** (Document [17]). Key characteristics include:

1. **Cyber-Biophysical Systems (Document [7])**:
   - **Type**: Hybrid systems integrating **biological, physical, and cyber components** (e.g., human-machine interfaces, wearable devices, or medical implants).
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Biological entities (e.g., human organs, tissues) or physical infrastructure (e.g., IoT sensors, actuators).
     - **Cyber Layer**: Embedded systems, edge/fog computing nodes, and real-time data acquisition units.
     - **Biophysical Interface**: Sensors (e.g., ECG, EEG, pressure sensors) and actuators (e.g., drug delivery systems, prosthetic controls).
   - **Operational Domain**: **Real-time monitoring, adaptive control, and predictive healthcare/industrial applications** (e.g., patient rehabilitation, industrial worker safety).
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Dynamic Interactions**: Bidirectional data flow between physical and digital domains (e.g., physiological signals → digital model → feedback to actuators).
     - **Multi-Scale Modeling**: Integration of **microscopic (cellular) to macroscopic (organ-level) scales** for cyber-biophysical simulations.
     - **Uncertainty Handling**: Incorporation of **stochastic processes** and **fuzzy logic** to account for biological variability.
     - **Regulatory Compliance**: Alignment with **medical device standards** (e.g., FDA, ISO 13485) for clinical applications.

2. **Automotive Systems (Documents [8], [9], [12], [14])**:
   - **Type**: **Connected and autonomous vehicles (CAVs)**, vehicle manufacturing lines, and **electrical/electronic (E/E) architectures**.
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Vehicles (e.g., powertrain, chassis, ADAS sensors), manufacturing robots, and supply chain logistics.
     - **Cyber Layer**: **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication**, embedded ECUs, and cloud-edge computing infrastructure.
     - **Digital Twin Components**: **High-fidelity digital models** (e.g., CAD, physics-based simulations) and **real-time telemetry streams**.
   - **Operational Domain**: **Design, testing, predictive maintenance, and autonomous driving** (e.g., collision avoidance, route optimization).
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Real-Time Synchronization**: **Sub-millisecond latency** requirements for V2X and autonomous driving applications (Document [8]).
     - **Multi-Domain Modeling**: Integration of **mechanical, electrical, thermal, and control systems** (e.g., using **Modelica** or **Simulink**).
     - **Data Fusion**: Combination of **sensor data (LiDAR, radar, cameras)**, **vehicle telemetry**, and **third-party data** (e.g., traffic, weather).
     - **Digital Thread**: End-to-end connectivity from **concept design to end-of-life recycling** (Document [10]).

3. **Manufacturing Systems (Document [10])**:
   - **Type**: **Industrial manufacturing processes** (e.g., CNC machining, assembly lines, additive manufacturing).
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Machines (e.g., lathes, 3D printers), robots, and production cells.
     - **Cyber Layer**: **Industrial IoT (IIoT) gateways**, PLCs, and **edge computing nodes** for local processing.
     - **Digital Twin Components**: **Digital representations of assets, processes, and supply chains**.
   - **Operational Domain**: **Process optimization, predictive maintenance, and quality control**.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **ISO 23247 Compliance**: Adherence to the **Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing** (Document [10]), including **lifecycle management** and **interoperability standards**.
     - **Multi-Scale Simulation**: From **component-level (e.g., tool wear)** to **plant-level (e.g., energy consumption)**.
     - **Closed-Loop Control**: Real-time adjustment of **production parameters** based on digital twin predictions.

4. **Aerospace Systems (Document [16])**:
   - **Type**: **Satellite systems** and **on-orbit collision avoidance**.
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Satellites, sensors (e.g., radar, optical trackers), and debris detection systems.
     - **Cyber Layer**: **Space-based computing nodes** and **ground station communication**.
     - **Digital Twin Components**: **High-fidelity orbital mechanics models** and **debris tracking simulations**.
   - **Operational Domain**: **Space situational awareness (SSA)** and **mission planning**.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Adaptation of ISO 23247**: Customization for **dynamic orbital environments** (e.g., variable gravitational forces, debris clouds).
     - **Real-Time Collision Prediction**: Integration with **NASA’s Space Debris Model** or **ESA’s ESADEB** for risk assessment.

5. **Battery Systems (Document [17])**:
   - **Type**: **Lithium-ion battery packs** (e.g., electric vehicle batteries, energy storage systems).
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Cells, modules, and battery management systems (BMS).
     - **Cyber Layer**: **In-situ sensors (voltage, temperature, SOC/SOH)** and **cloud-based analytics**.
     - **Digital Twin Components**: **Electrochemical models** (e.g., **P2D models**) and **thermal management simulations**.
   - **Operational Domain**: **Lifetime prediction, safety monitoring, and performance optimization**.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **ISO 23247 Alignment**: Application of the framework for **battery-specific digital twins** (e.g., **aging simulation, fault detection**).
     - **Multi-Physics Modeling**: Coupling of **electrochemical, thermal, and mechanical phenomena**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** systems described in the documents provide **domain-specific services** tailored to **monitoring, optimization, prediction, control, and visualization**, targeting **engineers, operators, decision-makers, and end-users**. Key services include:

1. **Cyber-Biophysical Systems (Document [7])**:
   - **Services**:
     - **Real-Time Monitoring**: Continuous **physiological parameter tracking** (e.g., heart rate, glucose levels) via **wearable sensors** and **embedded systems**.
     - **Predictive Analytics**: **Machine learning-driven health risk assessment** (e.g., early detection of chronic diseases).
     - **Adaptive Control**: **Closed-loop feedback** for **prosthetic devices** or **drug delivery systems** using **reinforcement learning**.
     - **Simulation-Based Training**: **Virtual patient simulators** for medical training (e.g., surgical planning).
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Healthcare providers** (doctors, nurses).
     - **Patients** (via mobile apps for self-monitoring).
     - **Researchers** (for hypothesis testing in cyber-biophysical interactions).
   - **Service Capabilities**:
     - **Uncertainty Quantification**: Integration of **Bayesian networks** or **Monte Carlo simulations** for probabilistic outcomes.
     - **Explainable AI**: **SHAP values** or **LIME** for interpreting model predictions in clinical decisions.

2. **Automotive Systems (Documents [8], [9], [14])**:
   - **Services**:
     - **Predictive Maintenance**: **Failure prediction** for **E/E components** (e.g., battery degradation, sensor drift) using **time-series forecasting (LSTM, Prophet)**.
     - **Traffic Optimization**: **Real-time route planning** for **autonomous vehicles** via **V2X communication** and **digital twin simulations**.
     - **Crash Simulation**: **High-fidelity collision modeling** (e.g., **LS-DYNA, ANSYS**) for **safety certification**.
     - **Energy Management**: **Battery state estimation** (e.g., **Kalman filters, particle filters**) and **charging optimization**.
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Automotive OEMs** (for design validation).
     - **Fleet operators** (for logistics optimization).
     - **Regulators** (for compliance testing).
   - **Service Capabilities**:
     - **Digital Shadow**: **Real-time synchronization** between physical and digital states with **<100ms latency** (Document [8]).
     - **What-If Analysis**: **Scenario testing** for **new traffic laws or cyber-attacks**.

3. **Manufacturing Systems (Document [10])**:
   - **Services**:
     - **Process Optimization**: **AI-driven parameter tuning** for **CNC machines** (e.g., **genetic algorithms, reinforcement learning**).
     - **Defect Detection**: **Computer vision + digital twin** for **surface flaw identification** in real-time.
     - **Supply Chain Simulation**: **End-to-end logistics modeling** (e.g., **AnyLogic, FlexSim**).
     - **Energy Efficiency**: **Predictive energy consumption modeling** for **factory optimization**.
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Manufacturing engineers**.
     - **Plant managers**.
     - **Supply chain analysts**.
   - **Service Capabilities**:
     - **Digital Thread Integration**: **Seamless data flow** from **CAD to MES (Manufacturing Execution System)**.
     - **ISO 23247 Compliance Services**: **Standardized digital twin lifecycle management**.

4. **Aerospace Systems (Document [16])**:
   - **Services**:
     - **Collision Avoidance**: **Real-time debris tracking** using **digital twin orbital models**.
     - **Mission Planning**: **Trajectory optimization** for **satellite maneuvers**.
     - **Anomaly Detection**: **AI-based fault diagnosis** for **spacecraft components**.
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Space agencies (NASA, ESA)**.
     - **Satellite operators**.
   - **Service Capabilities**:
     - **Dynamic Model Updates**: **Adaptive physics engines** for **variable gravitational fields**.

5. **Battery Systems (Document [17])**:
   - **Services**:
     - **Lifetime Prediction**: **Electrochemical aging models** (e.g., **P2D, EC-Lab**).
     - **Thermal Management**: **Real-time cooling optimization** via **digital twin simulations**.
     - **Fault Detection**: **Anomaly detection** for **internal short circuits or cell imbalance**.
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Battery manufacturers**.
     - **EV fleet operators**.
   - **Service Capabilities**:
     - **ISO 23247-Aligned Battery DT**: **Standardized data exchange** for **battery health monitoring**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **tooling and enablers** for Digital Twin (DT) systems in the provided documents include **software platforms, simulation engines, databases, edge/fog computing, and specialized technologies**. Key components are:

1. **Software Platforms and Frameworks (Documents [7], [10], [12], [14], [15])**:
   - **ISO 23247-Compliant Frameworks**:
     - **Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing (ISO 23247-1:2021)**: Provides **standardized architecture** for **manufacturing, automotive, and aerospace DTs** (Document [10]).
     - **Edge Computing-Based DT Framework (Document [14])**: Leverages **ISO 23247** with **edge nodes** for **low-latency processing** (e.g., **NVIDIA Jetson, Raspberry Pi clusters**).
   - **General-Purpose DT Platforms**:
     - **Siemens Teamcenter**: For **PLM (Product Lifecycle Management) integration**.
     - **PTC ThingWorx**: For **IIoT and real-time analytics**.
     - **Siemens MindSphere**: Cloud-based **DT-as-a-Service** for **automotive and industrial applications**.

2. **Simulation Engines and Modeling Tools (Documents [7], [8], [9], [15], [16], [17])**:
   - **Multi-Physics Simulation**:
     - **ANSYS**: For **structural, thermal, and fluid dynamics** (e.g., automotive crash testing, aerospace aerodynamics).
     - **LS-DYNA**: **Explicit finite element analysis** for **impact simulations**.
     - **Modelica**: **Physics-based modeling** for **electrical, mechanical, and thermal systems** (e.g., battery thermal management).
   - **AI/ML Integration**:
     - **TensorFlow/PyTorch**: For **predictive maintenance and anomaly detection**.
     - **Scikit-learn**: For **classification/regression tasks** in cyber-biophysical systems.
   - **Specialized Tools**:
     - **EC-Lab**: **Electrochemical modeling** for **battery DTs** (Document [17]).
     - **NASA’s STK (System Tool Kit)**: For **aerospace orbital mechanics simulations** (Document [16]).

3. **Databases and Data Management (Documents [7], [12], [14])**:
   - **Real-Time Databases**:
     - **InfluxDB**: For **time-series sensor data** (e.g., automotive telemetry, industrial IoT).
     - **MongoDB**: For **unstructured data storage** (e.g., cyber-biophysical logs).
   - **Digital Twin Databases**:
     - **Graph Databases (Neo4j)**: For **relationship modeling** (e.g., supply chain dependencies).
     - **Blockchain**: For **immutable audit trails** in **manufacturing DTs** (Document [12]).

4. **Edge/Fog Computing (Documents [7], [8], [14])**:
   - **Edge Nodes**:
     - **NVIDIA Jetson**: For **on-device AI inference** (e.g., autonomous vehicle perception).
     - **Intel FPGA**: For **low-latency control algorithms**.
   - **Fog Computing**:
     - **Microsoft Azure IoT Edge**: For **distributed DT processing** (e.g., industrial IoT).
     - **AWS Greengrass**: For **offline-capable DTs** in **aerospace applications**.

5. **Communication Protocols (Documents [8], [12], [14])**:
   - **V2X Communication**:
     - **5G/6G**: For **ultra-low latency** in **autonomous driving**.
     - **DDS (Data Distribution Service)**: For **real-time data sharing** in **aerospace DTs**.
   - **Industrial Protocols**:
     - **OPC UA**: For **manufacturing system interoperability**.
     - **MQTT**: For **lightweight IoT messaging** in **cyber-biophysical systems**.

6. **Specialized Technologies (Documents [7], [15], [16])**:
   - **Digital Twin Ontologies**:
     - **OWL/SWRL**: For **semantic reasoning** in **cyber-biophysical DTs**.
     - **ISO/IEC 30173**: **Standardized terminology** for **DT concepts** (Document [11]).
   - **Quantum Computing (Mentioned Implicitly)**:
     - Potential for **high-fidelity cyber-biophysical simulations** (Document [7]).
   - **Digital Twin-as-a-Service (DTaaS)**:
     - **Cloud-based DT platforms** (e.g., **Siemens MindSphere, PTC ThingWorx**) for **scalable deployment**.

7. **Development Tools (Documents [12], [15])**:
   - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**:
     - **SysML**: For **systems architecture modeling**.
     - **UML**: For **software component design**.
   - **Version Control**:
     - **Git + GitLab/GitHub**: For **collaborative DT development**.
   - **CI/CD Pipelines**:
     - **Jenkins**: For **automated DT deployment**.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces a **100 ms clock synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination between devices. This temporal constraint is mandated due to hardware limitations and aligns with safety-critical vehicular application requirements, though stricter standards (e.g., <100 ms) are referenced in related ISO 23247-based systems. The system’s temporal granularity is dictated by ROS2-based execution of Function Entities (FEs) as individual packages, where each FE’s latency and processing time are implicitly constrained by the 100 ms synchronization window. No explicit mention of service-specific time scales (e.g., real-time vs. batch processing) is provided beyond the hardware-imposed clock synchronization requirement.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system follows a **decentralized, entity-based architecture** with three primary hierarchical instances: the *Physical Twin Entity*, *Digital Twin Entity*, and *User Entity*. Each entity is deployed on **separate hardware components**, reflecting a departure from ISO 23247’s standard where Function Entities (FEs) are typically centralized or co-located. The *Physical Twin Entity* consolidates resource-specific functionality (e.g., sensors, actuators, compute) and general automotive features like *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*, while the *Digital Twin Entity* manages data management and control mechanisms. The *User Entity* hosts user-facing components (*3D Visualization* and *Control Panel*), split from the standard *User Interface FE*. No explicit mention of **multiple Digital Twin instances** (e.g., federated or replicated twins) or **coordination mechanisms** between them is provided; however, the decentralized deployment implies potential for inter-entity communication via ROS2’s distributed node architecture.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system adopts an **ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA)-inspired, entity-based orchestration pattern** with **ROS2** as the underlying middleware for component integration. Function Entities (FEs) are mapped to **individual ROS2 packages**, each containing one or more ROS2 nodes that implement FE functionality. This modularization enables **hardware-specific deployment** of entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) across distinct hardware components, deviating from ISO 23247’s co-located FE design. Resource management is implicit in the entity structure, where the *Physical Twin Entity* hosts both resource-specific (e.g., sensors) and general automotive functionalities (e.g., perception tasks). System-wide coordination relies on ROS2’s **publish-subscribe model** for inter-node communication, though explicit **orchestration protocols** (e.g., for failover or dynamic reconfiguration) are not detailed. The design emphasizes **engineering process alignment** with the entity-based structure, impacting ownership and scalability.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247** framework, which defines an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** where computational models and data are structured into **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**—physical or operational entities (e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel, or processes). The system employs **three primary entities** for data and model management:
1. **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**: Responsible for **real-time data acquisition** from OMEs via sensors and **device control** (e.g., actuation, monitoring). This entity interfaces with physical systems to ensure synchronized data flow.
2. **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**: Hosts **digital representations** of OMEs, including **geometric, behavioral, and analytical models** (e.g., CAD models for geometry, physics-based simulations for behavior, and analytical models for performance prediction). The DTE integrates these models into a **unified digital replica**, enabling virtual experimentation and analysis.
3. **User Entity**: Provides **human-machine interfaces** (HMIs) for interacting with the DTE, including visualization tools for monitoring data (e.g., operational metrics, sensor readings) and controlling OMEs via the Data Collection Entity.

**Data Sources**: Primarily derived from **physical sensors** embedded in OMEs, with supplementary data from **historical records** (e.g., maintenance logs, production data) and **external systems** (e.g., ERP/MES integrations). The system does not specify exact **sampling frequencies** or **data transmission protocols**, but the ISO 23247 implies reliance on **standardized information exchange mechanisms** (e.g., OPC UA, Industry 4.0 protocols) for interoperability.

**Model Relationships**:
- **Hierarchical decomposition**: OMEs are broken into **Functional Entities (FEs)**, which are modular components (e.g., data transformation, user interaction, or control logic). FEs are not standardized by ISO 23247 but may leverage **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** or similar middleware for implementation.
- **Bidirectional coupling**: The DTE maintains **real-time synchronization** with physical OMEs via the Data Collection Entity, ensuring models reflect current states. Analytical models (e.g., predictive maintenance algorithms) are embedded within the DTE to process data streams and generate insights.

**Data Management Approaches**:
- **Decentralized storage**: Each entity manages its own data (e.g., raw sensor data in the Data Collection Entity, processed models in the DTE).
- **Versioning and consistency**: The architecture implies mechanisms for **data reconciliation** to handle discrepancies between physical and digital states, though specific methods (e.g., conflict resolution algorithms) are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The **physical acting components** in this Digital Twin system are governed by the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which interfaces with **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** to enable **remote actuation and control**. Key technical details include:

1. **Actuator Types**: Not explicitly specified in the documents, but the system implies support for **standard industrial actuators** (e.g., motors, valves, robotic arms) used in manufacturing environments. These actuators are likely controlled via **OPC UA** or similar **industry-standard protocols** for device communication.

2. **Control Capabilities**:
   - **Real-time remote control**: The Data Collection Entity enables **bidirectional communication** with OMEs, allowing for **on-demand actuation** (e.g., starting/stopping machinery, adjusting parameters).
   - **Parameter tuning**: Actuators may support **dynamic reconfiguration** of operational parameters (e.g., speed, temperature) based on digital twin simulations or user inputs via the **User Entity**.

3. **Remote Control Interfaces**:
   - **Protocol-based**: Actuation commands are transmitted via **standardized protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, Modbus) to ensure interoperability with legacy and modern systems.
   - **Security constraints**: While not detailed, the ISO 23247 framework implies adherence to **industrial cybersecurity standards** (e.g., IEC 62443) to protect control channels from unauthorized access or tampering.

4. **Actuation Ranges and Limitations**:
   - **Physical constraints**: Actuators are constrained by their **mechanical/thermal limits** (e.g., maximum torque, operating temperature ranges), which must be validated against the digital twin’s analytical models to prevent malfunctions.
   - **Safety mechanisms**: The system likely incorporates **emergency stop protocols** and **fail-safe modes** (e.g., automatic shutdowns under fault conditions), though specific implementations are not described.

5. **Safety Constraints**:
   - **Hardware-level safeguards**: Actuators may include **built-in safety circuits** (e.g., overload protection, position sensors) to prevent damage.
   - **Digital twin validation**: Before actuation, the **Digital Twin Entity** may simulate potential outcomes (e.g., collision risks, energy consumption) to ensure safety compliance.

6. **Addressing Mechanisms**:
   - **Unique identifiers**: OMEs and actuators are likely assigned **standardized identifiers** (e.g., OPC UA Information Models) for unambiguous addressing in the system.
   - **Hierarchical addressing**: The **entity-based architecture** (Data Collection → DTE → User) ensures structured command routing, reducing latency and improving reliability.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The **physical sensing infrastructure** in this Digital Twin system is integral to the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which is responsible for **real-time data acquisition** from Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs). Key technical specifications include:

1. **Sensor Types**:
   - **Process sensors**: For monitoring operational parameters (e.g., temperature, pressure, vibration, flow rates) in manufacturing equipment.
   - **Position/state sensors**: Encoders, limit switches, or proximity sensors for tracking mechanical states (e.g., tool position, conveyor speed).
   - **Environmental sensors**: Humidity, air quality, or ambient temperature sensors for facility-level monitoring.
   - **Non-intrusive sensors**: Optical or acoustic sensors for indirect measurements (e.g., material properties, defect detection).

2. **Measurement Capabilities**:
   - **Physical quantities**: Primarily **continuous signals** (e.g., voltage, current, displacement) and **discrete events** (e.g., machine status changes).
   - **Resolution and range**: Not explicitly quantified in the documents, but sensors are assumed to meet **industrial-grade accuracy** (e.g., ±0.1% for temperature, ±0.5% for pressure) as per ISO 23247’s focus on manufacturing precision.

3. **Spatial Distribution**:
   - **Embedded sensors**: Directly integrated into OMEs (e.g., embedded in machinery, tools, or products).
   - **Distributed network**: Sensors are deployed across the **entire manufacturing footprint**, including **critical nodes** (e.g., production lines, storage areas) and **peripheral zones** (e.g., logistics, maintenance areas).
   - **Hierarchical placement**: Sensors may follow a **pyramidal structure** (local → zone → plant level) to optimize data granularity and transmission efficiency.

4. **Sampling Frequencies**:
   - **High-frequency sensors**: Critical parameters (e.g., vibration, torque) may be sampled at **kilohertz ranges** (e.g., 1–10 kHz) for real-time diagnostics.
   - **Low-frequency sensors**: Non-critical metrics (e.g., temperature, humidity) typically sampled at **seconds to minutes** (e.g., 1 Hz to 0.1 Hz).
   - **Event-triggered sampling**: Some sensors may activate on **asynchronous events** (e.g., alarms, anomalies) to reduce unnecessary data load.

5. **Data Transmission Rates**:
   - **Wireless/ethernet**: Sensors transmit data via **industrial Ethernet** (e.g., Profibus, EtherCAT) or **wireless protocols** (e.g., IEEE 802.15.4, LoRa) with **latency constraints** (e.g., <100 ms for critical paths).
   - **Bandwidth optimization**: Compression algorithms (e.g., lossless for raw data, lossy for non-critical streams) may be applied to manage network congestion.

6. **Accuracy and Precision Specifications**:
   - **Calibration**: Sensors are assumed to undergo **periodic calibration** (e.g., monthly/quarterly) to maintain **ISO/IEC 17025 compliance** (though not explicitly stated).
   - **Error margins**: Typical industrial tolerances (e.g., **±0.5% for strain gauges**, **±1°C for RTDs**) are implied, with **uncertainty quantification** handled via the Digital Twin Entity’s validation processes.
   - **Cross-validation**: Multiple sensors may redundantly measure the same parameter to **detect outliers** or **sensor failures**.

7. **Data Collection Mechanisms**:
   - **Pull-based**: The Data Collection Entity **actively queries** sensors at predefined intervals (e.g., via OPC UA subscriptions).
   - **Push-based**: Sensors may **asynchronously push** data to the Data Collection Entity upon detecting thresholds or anomalies.
   - **Edge processing**: Some sensors may perform **local filtering/aggregation** (e.g., averaging, noise reduction) before transmission to reduce cloud/edge server load.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The **fidelity and validity** of the Digital Twin system are governed by the **ISO 23247 framework**, which emphasizes **model accuracy, validation, and uncertainty management** across its entity-based architecture. Key technical considerations include:

1. **Model Fidelity Requirements**:
   - **Geometric fidelity**: Digital representations of OMEs must match **CAD models** with **sub-millimeter precision** for critical components (e.g., tooling, molds).
   - **Behavioral fidelity**: **Physics-based simulations** (e.g., finite element analysis, fluid dynamics) are embedded in the **Digital Twin Entity** to replicate real-world dynamics (e.g., thermal expansion, structural stress).
   - **Analytical fidelity**: Predictive models (e.g., machine learning, regression algorithms) must achieve **>90% accuracy** in forecasting operational metrics (e.g., downtime, energy consumption) based on historical and real-time data.

2. **Validation and Verification Methods**:
   - **Comparative validation**: Digital twin outputs are **continuously compared** against physical sensor data via the Data Collection Entity to detect discrepancies.
   - **Benchmarking**: Models are validated against **industry standards** (e.g., ANSI/ISA-95 for manufacturing processes) and **historical performance data**.
   - **Certification**: The system may undergo **third-party audits** (e.g., ISO 9001 compliance) to ensure model integrity, though specifics are not provided.

3. **Uncertainty Sources and Quantification**:
   - **Sensor noise**: **Random and systematic errors** in sensors are quantified via **statistical analysis** (e.g., confidence intervals, root-mean-square error).
   - **Model approximations**: Simplifications in analytical models (e.g., linearization of nonlinear dynamics) introduce **bounded errors**, which are mitigated through **sensitivity analysis**.
   - **Environmental factors**: External variables (e.g., temperature drift, humidity) are accounted for via **uncertainty propagation models** integrated into the Digital Twin Entity.

4. **Error Detection and Correction Mechanisms**:
   - **Anomaly detection**: The system employs **statistical process control (SPC)** and **machine learning algorithms** to identify outliers in sensor data or model predictions.
   - **Automated recalibration**: Digital twin models may **self-correct** by adjusting parameters based on real-time feedback loops (e.g., recalibrating sensor offsets, updating predictive coefficients).
   - **Human-in-the-loop**: The **User Entity** provides interfaces for manual intervention (e.g., overriding model predictions, triggering recalibration).

5. **Quality Assurance Processes**:
   - **Continuous monitoring**: The **Digital Twin Entity** logs **model performance metrics** (e.g., prediction error rates, data consistency scores) for auditing.
   - **Version control**: Changes to models or data sources are tracked via **configuration management systems** to ensure traceability.
   - **Compliance checks**: The system adheres to **industrial standards** (e.g., IEC 62264 for enterprise integration) to ensure data and model validity across the lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves real-time data acquisition from physical sensors and preprocessing to generate actionable digital representations. **Data flows** originate from LiDAR, motor, steering servo, and vehicle state sensors, which transmit raw inputs (e.g., point clouds, motor actuation signals, steering servo angles, and vehicle state telemetry) to the **Data Pre-Processing Functional Entity (FE)**. The **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** SW component processes raw LiDAR data (2D/3D point clouds) into structured object tracks and environmental maps, ensuring spatial alignment and noise reduction via clustering algorithms. Vehicle state data (e.g., speed, acceleration, yaw rate) and actuation signals (motor/servo commands) are also preprocessed for consistency and synchronization.

**Communication protocols** include **ROS2** for intra-system data exchange and **UDP over Wi-Fi** for inter-entity communication, with **serialization** and **unit conversion** handled by the **Data Translation FE** to standardize message formats. **Transmission frequencies** are not explicitly stated, but real-time monitoring implies high-frequency updates (e.g., 10–100 Hz for LiDAR/state data). **Data quality assurance** is implied through preprocessing (e.g., clustering, tracking) and visualization validation (e.g., 3D path projection in Fig. 3a). **Integration mechanisms** rely on ROS2 topics for topic-based pub/sub communication, ensuring decoupled yet synchronized data streams between physical sensors and digital twins.

**Event triggers** include sensor activations (e.g., LiDAR scans, motor feedback) and system events (e.g., vehicle state changes), which are mapped to digital twin updates via the **Data Collecting FE** and **Presentation FE**. **Data storage** occurs post-processing for reporting, with raw and processed data archived for analytics.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this DTS is facilitated by the **Controlling FE**, which translates digital commands into physical actuation signals. **Control flows** originate from the digital twin’s **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) system**, where projected driving paths and object tracks (derived from LiDAR data) are processed into **control signals** for motor and steering servo actuation. **Command types** include:
- **Motor Actuation Commands**: PWM or torque-based signals (serialized via Data Translation FE).
- **Steering Servo Commands**: Angle or position commands (aligned to physical servo limits).
- **Vehicle State Adjustments**: Dynamic feedback loops (e.g., speed corrections) based on real-time digital twin projections.

**Control message formats** adhere to ROS2 message structures, with **validation** performed by the **Data Translation FE** to ensure unit consistency (e.g., m/s → km/h conversions) and **authentication** implied via secure UDP communication channels. **Feedback systems** rely on bidirectional data flows: physical sensor inputs (e.g., LiDAR, vehicle state) update the digital twin, while the twin’s **Controlling FE** generates corrective commands (e.g., braking, lane adjustments) via the **Actuation FE**.

**Conflict resolution** is not explicitly detailed, but the system’s reliance on **real-time synchronization** (ROS2 + UDP) suggests prioritization of the most recent valid command. **Command execution confirmation** is inferred through closed-loop feedback: the digital twin validates actuation outcomes by comparing predicted (digital) and observed (physical) states (e.g., via LiDAR recalibration or vehicle telemetry).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s Technical Connection** is built on a **multi-layered network architecture** combining **ROS2** for intra-system communication and **UDP over Wi-Fi** for inter-entity interactions. **Communication protocols** include:
- **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)**: Used for intra-DTS communication between Functional Entities (FEs), enabling **topic-based pub/sub** for data streams (e.g., LiDAR point clouds, control signals).
- **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)**: Employed for inter-entity communication (e.g., Physical Twin → Digital Twin) over Wi-Fi, prioritizing **low-latency** over reliability (no built-in retransmission).

**Network topology** follows a **hybrid client-server model**: the Digital Twin Entity acts as a central hub processing data from Physical Twin sensors (LiDAR, motor/servo) and User Entity inputs (e.g., control panel commands). **Bandwidth requirements** are not quantified, but real-time 3D visualization (Fig. 3a) and LiDAR processing imply **high-throughput** needs (≥10 Mbps for LiDAR data streams). **Latency requirements** are critical for control loops (e.g., ACC), with UDP’s inherent **<100 ms** latency suitable for this use case.

**Reliability mechanisms** include:
- **ROS2’s built-in QoS policies** (e.g., history depth, reliability settings) to handle packet loss.
- **Redundancy** is not explicitly mentioned, but the system’s reliance on **real-time synchronization** suggests tolerance for minor delays via buffering in the Data Translation FE.
**Security protocols** are implied but not detailed; however, UDP’s stateless nature and Wi-Fi’s lack of encryption suggest potential vulnerabilities. **Data Translation FE** ensures message integrity via serialization/deserialization, but end-to-end encryption is **Not in Document**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure leverages a **distributed, edge-centric model** with **on-premise deployment** for low-latency processing. **Hosting platforms** include:
- **On-Premise Servers**: Hosting the Digital Twin Entity’s core components (e.g., Data Translation FE, Controlling FE) to minimize cloud dependency and ensure **real-time responsiveness** (critical for ACC).
- **Edge Devices**: Embedded systems (e.g., vehicle ECUs) for **Data Pre-Processing FE** and **Actuation FE**, reducing cloud latency for sensor data (LiDAR, motor/servo).

**Computational requirements** are high due to:
- **Point Cloud Processing**: Clustering/tracking algorithms (e.g., DBSCAN, Kalman filtering) demand **GPU acceleration** (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson or high-end CPUs).
- **3D Visualization**: Real-time rendering of vehicle environments (Fig. 3a) requires **dedicated graphics processing** (e.g., OpenGL/Vulkan).
- **Control Logic**: ACC algorithms (e.g., path planning, obstacle avoidance) necessitate **real-time OS support** (ROS2’s deterministic scheduling).

**Storage requirements** include:
- **Raw Data Storage**: LiDAR point clouds and sensor logs (e.g., vehicle state telemetry) stored for reporting (via **Reporting FE**).
- **Processed Data Storage**: Object tracks, environmental maps, and control trajectories (archived for analytics).

**Scalability mechanisms** are **Not in Document**, but the system’s modular ROS2 architecture allows horizontal scaling of FEs (e.g., adding more LiDAR processing nodes). **Deployment models supported** include:
1. **Single-Node Deployment**: All FEs co-located on a high-performance edge server.
2. **Distributed Deployment**: FEs partitioned across edge devices (e.g., LiDAR processing on a dedicated GPU node).

**Availability/reliability assurance** relies on:
- **Redundant Sensor Inputs**: Multiple LiDAR/motor sensors (implied by clustering/tracking robustness).
- **ROS2’s Fault Tolerance**: Automatic recovery from node failures via topic-based retries.
- **No Cloud Dependency**: On-premise deployment eliminates single points of failure (e.g., cloud outages).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The ISO 23247 standard outlines a **reference architecture (RA)** for Digital Twins (DTs) in manufacturing, structured around **entity-based components** with defined roles:
    - **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**: Physical items (equipment, facilities, products, personnel, processes) that are observable or controllable.
    - **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**: Responsible for **data acquisition** from OMEs and **control** of physical operations.
    - **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**: The **digital representation** of the OME, implementing its virtual counterpart.
    - **User Entity**: Provides **interfaces** for users to interact with the DTE (e.g., monitoring data or controlling OMEs).

    The standard does not explicitly detail **development stages** (e.g., conceptualization, deployment, retirement) or **transition management** between phases. However, the **entity-based RA** implies modular development, where **Functional Entities (FEs)**—smaller units for specific tasks (e.g., data transformation, user interaction)—can be developed iteratively. The standard suggests **flexibility in implementation** (e.g., using OPC UA for interoperability) but lacks explicit **version control** or **evolution management** strategies. Governance approaches are not addressed in the provided text.

    **Key Implication**: The architecture emphasizes **modularity** and **interoperability** but does not prescribe lifecycle governance or evolution pathways beyond entity-based design.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The document does not provide explicit details on **decision support capabilities**, **analytics processing**, or **user interaction methods** for the Digital Twin. While the **User Entity** is described as enabling interaction (e.g., monitoring or control), no mention is made of:
    - Types of **insights** generated (predictive, prescriptive, etc.).
    - **Analytics capabilities** (e.g., real-time processing, machine learning).
    - **Decision-making processes** or confidence measures for recommendations.
    - **Human-machine interfaces** (e.g., dashboards, alerts).
    **Conclusion**: These aspects are **not in Document**.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The document explicitly references the **ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing** standard (2021), which includes:
    - **Reference Architecture (Part 2)**: Defines an **entity-based RA** with roles for OMEs, Data Collection/Control Entities, Digital Twin Entities, and User Entities.
    - **Information Exchange (Part 4)**: Implies standards for **data interoperability**, though specifics are not detailed.
    - **Certification/Compliance**: Not mentioned, but the standard itself is an **industry-recognized framework** for DTs in manufacturing.
    - **Interoperability Standards**: The document suggests **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** as a potential implementation choice for connecting entities, though it is not mandated by the standard.

    **Key Points**:
    - **Compliance Framework**: ISO 23247 serves as the foundational standard.
    - **Interoperability**: OPC UA is implied as a **de facto** standard for communication between entities.
    - **Lack of Detail**: No mention of **certification requirements** or **additional compliance frameworks** (e.g., IEC 62424 for IoT).
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The security and safety considerations for the Digital Twin system, as referenced in the provided documents, emphasize **functional safety** and **cybersecurity protocols** aligned with automotive and vehicular communication standards. Specifically:

1. **Functional Safety (ISO 26262-1:2018)**:
   - Adherence to **ISO 26262**, the international standard for functional safety in road vehicles, mandates a structured approach to managing risks in safety-critical systems. This includes:
     - **Automotive Safety Integrity Levels (ASIL)** classification to assess risk levels and determine safety requirements.
     - **Life Cycle Processes** (e.g., concept phase, development, production, operation, service, and decommissioning) with defined safety activities and documentation.
     - **Risk Assessment and Mitigation**: Systematic identification, analysis, and mitigation of hazards using techniques like **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** and **FTA (Fault Tree Analysis)**.
     - **Safety Mechanisms**: Implementation of **fail-safe designs**, redundancy, and **watchdog timers** to detect and recover from faults in real-time.

2. **Cybersecurity Protocols (3GPP TS 22.185 and ISO 15622/ISO 15622:2018)**:
   - **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) Security**: The **3GPP TS 22.185** specification outlines security requirements for V2X services, including:
     - **Authentication and Authorization**: Use of **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** for device authentication, ensuring only authorized entities (e.g., vehicles, infrastructure) can communicate.
     - **Confidentiality and Integrity**: Encryption of messages using **AES-128** or **AES-256** in **IPsec** or **TSG (Transport Security Gateway)** frameworks to protect against eavesdropping and tampering.
     - **Secure Communication Channels**: Mandatory use of **ITS-G5** (Intelligent Transport Systems Global System for Mobile Communications) or **C-V2X (Cellular Vehicle-to-Everything)** with **5G SA (Standalone)** for low-latency, high-reliability communication.
     - **Denial-of-Service (DoS) Mitigation**: Deployment of **rate limiting**, **anomaly detection**, and **firewall policies** to prevent malicious flooding or jamming attacks.
   - **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) Security (ISO 15622:2018)**:
     - **Safety-Critical Data Protection**: Ensures that sensor data (e.g., radar, LiDAR) and control commands are protected against unauthorized access or manipulation.
     - **Fail-Safe Mechanisms**: Integration of **emergency stop protocols** and **redundant control systems** to maintain operational safety during cyber incidents.

3. **Threat Mitigation Strategies**:
   - **Real-Time Monitoring**: Continuous logging and analysis of system behavior to detect deviations from expected patterns (e.g., using **intrusion detection systems (IDS)**).
   - **Patch Management**: Regular updates to firmware and software to address vulnerabilities (e.g., **OTA updates** for embedded systems).
   - **Isolation and Sandboxing**: Segmentation of critical systems (e.g., **hardware security modules (HSM)**) to limit lateral movement of threats.

4. **Risk Assessment Approaches**:
   - **Quantitative Risk Analysis (QRA)**: Use of probabilistic models to assess the likelihood and impact of cyber-physical failures.
   - **Safety Case Documentation**: Formal justification of safety and security measures, including **traceability matrices** linking requirements to design elements and test results.

5. **Safety-Critical System Considerations**:
   - **Deterministic Communication**: Guaranteed latency and bandwidth for time-sensitive applications (e.g., **5G URLLC - Ultra-Reliable Low Latency Communication**).
   - **Redundancy and Diversity**: Deployment of multiple sensors and controllers with **cross-verification** to ensure consistency in decision-making.
   - **Human-Machine Interface (HMI) Safety**: Clear **alert protocols** and **user feedback mechanisms** to inform operators of system status or failures (as hinted in [37] for interactive digital twin cockpits).

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a.
